nativeRange.habitat: Rivers, streams, and flooded fields; inhabits midwater and near-surface zones of clear, moderately fast-flowing rivers with sandy or gravelly substrates and riparian vegetation

decorPreferences: fine-leaved plants (hornwort, Vallisneria, Cabomba) along the margins
dark or natural substrate to highlight silver coloration

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of prepared and live foods. Offer high-quality flake or micro-pellets as a staple, supplemented with frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, daphnia, and brine shrimp. Wild fish feed primarily on algae, small invertebrates, and organic detritus; including spirulina-based flake or blanched vegetables rounds out the diet. Feed small amounts two to three times daily.

goodWith: similarly sized peaceful cyprinids (danios, other rasboras)
avoidWith: very small fish or dwarf shrimp (fry predation risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species thumbnail for Rasbora argyrotaenia. Verify dimensions and licensing before uploading to R2.

summary: The Silver Rasbora is a larger schooling cyprinid from Southeast Asia reaching up to 17 cm, displaying an elongated silver body with a faint reddish mid-lateral stripe. Hardy and active, it thrives in groups of eight or more in spacious, well-planted tanks with a secure lid — it is a confirmed jumper.
careNotes:
Rasbora argyrotaenia is one of the larger members of the genus, commonly reaching 12–17 cm in aquaria — considerably bigger than the small “nano” rasboras popular in planted tanks. A tank of at least 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is recommended to accommodate a proper shoal of eight or more fish and give them space to display natural midwater schooling behaviour. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–10), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.5–7.5), and well-oxygenated; the species originates from flowing river environments and appreciates moderate current from a power filter or spray bar. Temperature 20–26°C suits the species; the lower end of this range is tolerable but temperatures above 26°C should be avoided long-term.

Escape risk is high: the Silver Rasbora is a strong and active jumper, and a tight-fitting lid with no gaps larger than a few millimetres is essential. Losses to jumping are commonly reported in tanks with loosely fitting covers or light strips that leave open sections.

Decor should include open midwater swimming space flanked by fine-leaved plants and driftwood along the tank edges. A dark or natural substrate enhances the species’ silver and red colouration. The Silver Rasbora is peaceful and makes an excellent dither fish in community setups with loaches, medium tetras, corydoras, and other cyprinids of comparable size. Keep in groups of at least eight — smaller numbers increase stress and lead to subdued colouration and erratic behaviour. Diet is undemanding; quality flake, micro-pellets, and regular frozen or live supplementation (bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp) maintain condition. The species is widely kept in Southeast Asia both as an ornamental fish and as a food fish, contributing to its familiarity in the regional trade.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; breeding in captivity is possible but infrequently attempted by hobbyists given the species’ larger size. Separate a well-conditioned pair or small group into a bare breeding tank (100+ L) with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops and very soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.5, gH 2–4, temperature raised to 26°C). Courtship involves the male chasing the female vigorously. Eggs are non-adhesive and scattered among plants; parents will eat eggs and fry if not removed immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–30 hours at 26°C; fry become free-swimming within 2–3 days and initially accept infusoria or commercial fry food, transitioning to baby brine shrimp and micro-worms as they grow. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the need for a separate setup and triggered conditioning.

primary.name: FishBase — Rasbora argyrotaenia
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (max 14.0 cm TL), temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.5–7.0, hardness up to 10 dH, native range (Mekong/Chao Phraya/Mae Khlong basins, Malay Peninsula, Borneo, Java, Sumatra), habitat (rivers and flooded fields, benthopelagic), diet (algae/primary consumer, trophic level 2.0), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes, subfamily Rasborinae). Adult size extended to 17 cm per user brief citing hobbyist sources and regional aquaculture data; 12 cm used as minimum from FishBase male/unsexed maximum.

additional.name: FishBase Common Names — Rasbora argyrotaenia
additional.notes: Confirmed common English trade name ‘Silver rasbora’ (AFS, FAO, Australian designations). Provided regional vernacular names: Seluang / Buntung / Londoi (Malay), Lunjar pareh (Javanese), Silberbärbling (German), Sølvrasbora (Danish). Synonyms noted: Leuciscus argyrotaenia, Rasbora everetti, Rasbora vaillanti.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Silver rasbora
additional.notes: Confirmed geographic distribution (Mekong, Chao Phraya, Mae Klong basins, Malay Peninsula, Borneo, Java, Sumatra), taxonomy, and IUCN Least Concern status. Article is a stub; limited aquarium care detail.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Top 10 Rasboras
additional.notes: Rasbora argyrotaenia not individually covered. Used for general large-rasbora care guidance: tank footprint (minimum 4 ft / 120 cm for scissortail-class rasboras), diet (prepared flake + frozen bloodworms/brine shrimp), pH 6–8, temperature 23–25°C, and secure-lid escape risk for active schooling species.

additional.name: ResearchGate — Pemijahan dan Perkembangan Awal Larva Ikan Rasbora (Rasbora argyrotaenia)
additional.notes: Indonesian-language spawning study. Confirmed egg-scattering breeding mode, 24–30 h hatch time at 26°C, fertilisation rate ~95%, hatching rate ~82%. Breeding triggered with Ovaprim; spawning ratio 1 male : 1 female. Used to populate breedingNotes.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; separate breeding tank with soft acidic water and spawning mops required. Parents eat eggs; remove immediately after spawning. Hatch in ~24–30 h at 26°C.
